title: Understanding food date labels and using sensory checks to reduce waste
subtitle: The legal difference between best before and use by, and how to assess most food by smell, appearance, and texture
excerpt: Best-before dates indicate quality, not safety. Use-by dates indicate safety. Most foods with a best-before date are safe to eat after the date has passed if stored correctly and assessed by sensory check. This guide covers the legal definition of each label type, which categories of food have genuine safety risks, and the practical sensory assessment that professional food buyers use as a matter of routine.
sourceNotes: UK Food Standards Agency guidance on date labels. Food Information to Consumers Regulation (EU) 1169/2011 as retained in UK law. WRAP UK Food Waste Reduction Roadmap. REFRESH project research on consumer date label confusion. gov.uk guidance on food labelling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A safety date. Food must not be eaten after the use-by date regardless of appearance or smell, because harmful bacteria may have grown without any visible or olfactory sign. In UK law, retailers must not sell food past its use-by date.

A quality date. Food after a best-before date may have deteriorated in texture, colour, or flavour, but is not necessarily unsafe to eat if stored correctly. In UK law, retailers may sell food past its best-before date.

content.text: The UK throws away around 9.5 million tonnes of food per year. The Food Standards Agency estimates that date label confusion accounts for around 250,000 tonnes of that total: food with a
discarded because a household handles it the same way as a
Fresh chilled meat (including deli products): do not eat after use-by.
Fresh fish and shellfish: do not eat after use-by.
Some ready-made chilled meals and chilled dairy products.
Dried pasta, rice, lentils, and pulses: safe for 1 to 2 years past best-before if stored dry.
Canned food: safe for years past best-before. If the can is undamaged and not swollen, the contents are safe.
Flour, sugar, salt: no safety risk past best-before. The only change is quality. Flour loses some raising power over time; that is not a reason to throw it away.
Hard cheese: surface mould can be cut off (at least 1 cm around the mould) and the rest eaten. Not true for soft cheese, which should be discarded if mouldy.
Bread: surface mould indicates it should be discarded. Stale bread without mould is safe and good for breadcrumbs, bread pudding, or toast.

For foods with a best-before date: look, smell, taste a small amount if the first two pass. Off flavours are detectable before harmful levels of most spoilage organisms are reached. This is not the same as use-by foods where pathogenic bacteria (Listeria, Salmonella, E. coli O157) can be present at dangerous levels without detectable smell or taste change. Never use sensory checks as a substitute for the use-by date on safety-relevant products.

attrs.symptom: Eggs: no date visible or past best before

content.text: Float test: a fresh egg sinks horizontally. An egg that stands on one end is 1 to 2 weeks old but still safe. An egg that floats is stale and should be discarded. This works because the air cell enlarges as the egg ages.
